topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of old paper and stale coffee suddenly hit me as I opened my desk drawer. My heart gave a hard thump against my ribs. Tucked beneath a stack of textbooks, I found it: my journal. The handwriting, my own looping script, filled the pages, detailing late-night anxieties about exams and vague dreams of escape. I stared, my jaw tightening. I knew, with a sickening lurch in my stomach, that Liam had been reading it. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and walked toward his room. When I knocked, the silence that followed was heavy, thick with unspoken things. He opened the door, a lazy smile playing on his lips, and the casualness of his demeanor made my hands tremble as I held up the book.

[story 2]

I found the evidence while tidying up the shared space. My breath hitched as I recognized the familiar ink bleeding across the pages of my diary. Every entry, every secret worry I’d poured onto those sheets, was there, meticulously transcribed. A wave of intense annoyance washed over me, making my shoulders tense up near my ears. I clenched my fists so tightly my nails dug into my palms. I didn't want to confront him; the thought of an argument felt exhausting. Instead, I dropped the journal onto his bedside table with a sharp *thud*, the sound echoing unnervingly in the quiet room. I turned and walked away quickly, my pace quick and jerky, needing distance from the suffocating feeling building in my chest.

[story 3]

It was the quietest Tuesday afternoon, the kind where the only sound was the hum of the refrigerator. I was organizing my bookshelf when I noticed the gap on my desk. My journal, the one I kept locked away, was gone. Panic flared, hot and sharp, making my skin prickle. Then I saw it—a small, slightly crumpled corner of the page tucked behind a loose book. It was my handwriting, and beside it, a faint, smudged note that wasn't mine. My eyes darted toward Liam’s room, a slow, deliberate movement. I felt a deep, simmering irritation rising in my throat, a pressure that made me want to shout, but I kept my voice low, dangerously steady. I waited by the door, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame, unwilling to let the situation escalate into a messy confrontation.
